perturbation.original_text: eDiets shall ensure that the eDiets Content complies with Women.com's editorial guidelines. Women.com will provide eDiets with specific editorial guidelines for eDiets Content. Women.com reserves the right to reject any and all content that does not meet Women.com's editorial guidelines. eDiets will then be required to edit such 2 content until all content provided by eDiets complies with Women.com's editorial guidelines and standards.
perturbation.changed_text: eDiets shall endeavor to ensure that the eDiets Content generally aligns with Women.com's editorial preferences. Women.com may provide eDiets with suggested editorial guidelines for eDiets Content. Women.com retains the option to express concerns regarding content that may not fully align with Women.com's editorial preferences. eDiets will then consider revising such content based on Women.com’s expression of concerns.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall ensure that the eDiets Content complies' with 'shall endeavor to ensure that the eDiets Content generally aligns,' weakening the obligation. 'Women.com's editorial guidelines' is replaced with 'Women.com's editorial preferences,' making the standards subjective. The mandatory editing requirement is replaced with a discretionary 'consider revising,' creating uncertainty regarding content compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. By weakening the obligation to ensure factual accuracy and compliance with editorial guidelines, the modified text could allow for the dissemination of misleading information, thus contradicting this law. The original text held eDiets strictly responsible for accuracy per Women.com's editorial guidelines, while the modified text only requires a general alignment, opening the door for potentially misleading content to be published.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17500, which prohibits false or misleading advertising. The original text required strict compliance with Women.com's editorial guidelines, ensuring accuracy. The changed text weakens this obligation to a general alignment with Women.com's editorial preferences, potentially allowing for misleading content. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association...to make or disseminate...any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services...any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo,' which the modified agreement risks violating due to its ambiguous standards.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to use the Confidential Information of the other party solely to the extent necessary to fulfill its obligations or exercise its rights hereunder, and not for any other purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will generally use the Confidential Information of the other party to the extent deemed reasonably helpful in fulfilling its obligations or exercising its rights hereunder, with consideration given to other business purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The change introduces ambiguity by replacing the strict 'solely to the extent necessary' with 'to the extent deemed reasonably helpful,' making the usage of confidential information more discretionary. The addition of 'with consideration given to other business purposes' further broadens the scope and creates uncertainty regarding the permitted use of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets and confidential information from misappropriation. The modified text, by allowing use of confidential information for purposes beyond fulfilling contractual obligations, creates a risk of misappropriation, potentially violating CUTSA. The original text was designed to prevent this from happening, while the changes weakens the overall confidentiality surrounding trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens confidentiality by allowing the use of confidential information for purposes beyond fulfilling contractual obligations, potentially violating the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The original text restricts use to 'solely to the extent necessary', while the changed text allows use 'to the extent deemed reasonably helpful' and 'with consideration given to other business purposes'. Snippet 1 confirms that Title 5 of the Civil Code covers the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, suggesting that the changes weaken the overall confidentiality surrounding trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Women.com shall make advertising reports generated by NetGravity available to eDiets either online or in some other mutually agreed upon format. Such reports shall contain the number of impressions and other metrics attributable to each general category of advertising specified in Exhibit B hereto, as well as such other information as eDiets may reasonably request, to the extent reasonable commercial efforts and current technology permit.
perturbation.changed_text: Women.com will try to provide advertising reports that are generally aligned with NetGravity data to eDiets when feasible. These reports will attempt to include the number of impressions and some metrics for major advertising categories outlined in Exhibit B, along with other information as eDiets may ask for, depending on Women.com's resources and technical capabilities.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces the firm obligation of 'shall make available' with the weaker 'will try to provide,' making the provision of reports discretionary. 'Shall contain' is replaced with 'will attempt to include,' making the content of the reports uncertain. The clear requirements of the original are now conditional depending on subjective judgements such as 'when feasible' and subjective limitations like 'depending on Women.com's resources'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California False Advertising Law (Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §§ 17500)
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the advertising reports less reliable and comprehensive, Women.com could potentially misrepresent the value of the advertising services provided. This could constitute false advertising under California Business and Professions Code § 17500, which prohibits disseminating untrue or misleading statements regarding services offered. The original text was very clear about requiring comprehensive reports, but now, the reports may contain missing or inaccurate advertising data.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17500, which prohibits disseminating untrue or misleading statements regarding services offered. The original contract language, 'shall make available' and 'shall contain,' imposes a firm obligation to provide accurate and comprehensive advertising reports. The changed text weakens this obligation by using phrases like 'will try to provide' and 'will attempt to include,' making the provision of reports discretionary and the content uncertain. This directly contradicts the law, as the modified text now allows for potentially incomplete or inaccurate advertising data, which could mislead eDiets regarding the value of the advertising services. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'It is unlawful... to make or disseminate... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services...' The modified text could lead to Women.com disseminating statements that are not fully accurate or comprehensive, violating this law.
